exemplar.title: An Investigation Into The Anchorng Effect

detailedExemplarData.title: An Investigation Into The Anchorng Effect

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The investigation aim is clearly stated and linked to real-world decision-making biases.
Anchoring effect is accurately described with reference to Tversky & Kahneman and Strack & Mussweiler.
Independent and dependent variables are correctly identified and operationalized in the hypothesis.
weaknesses: Terminology confusion occurs (calling anchoring a âtheoryâ rather than a bias).
The Strack & Mussweiler description is cut off mid-sentence, leaving procedural details incomplete.
Relevance discussion is rather brief and could better justify why marketing and finance domains are especially susceptible.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the introduction demonstrates a clear, well-connected aim, accurate model description, and operationalized variables, minor lapsesâsuch as terminology imprecision and an incomplete literature descriptionâlimit it to the mid-band (4/6). The relevance is explained but not in sufficient depth to warrant higher marks.

strengths: Independent-samples design is not only described but also its choice explained to avoid carry-over effects.
Opportunity sampling is justified with reference to language fluency and participant availability.
Controlled variables (room, instructions, time limit) and materials are identified with brief rationale.
weaknesses: Justification omits discussion of alternative designs (e.g., matched-pairs) to strengthen methodological choices.
Sampling bias (representativeness of pre-IB students) is acknowledged but not fully explored.
Ethical procedures (debriefing, withdrawal rights) are mentioned only superficially.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section meets the descriptor for a Band 3/4 performance: research design, sampling and materials are explained, but the treatment of alternative methods, sampling bias, and ethics is not sufficiently comprehensive to reach the top of the band. A score of 3/4 reflects this balance.

strengths: Appropriate non-parametric test (Mann-Whitney U) is selected for two independent groups with small N.
Graphical presentation addresses the hypothesis with correct chart type and error bars.
Findings are interpreted and linked back to the anchoring hypothesis, showing clear conceptual connection.
weaknesses: There is a contradiction in reported critical U values (33 vs 38) indicating reporting errors.
Exact p-values, test assumptions, and measures of effect size are omitted, reducing statistical rigor.
Table and figure formatting lack full labels (axis units, clear headers), hindering clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical methods and interpretation are broadly appropriate, but calculation inconsistencies, missing inferential details, and presentation flaws restrict this section to a mid-band performance. A score of 3/6 reflects solid application marred by notable errors and omissions.

strengths: Findings are connected to background theory (Kahneman & Tversky, Strack & Mussweiler).
Strengths and limitations are clearly stated and directly relevant to the investigationâs validity.
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and are logically justified.
weaknesses: Discussion lacks critical depthâalternative explanations or boundary conditions are not explored.
Quantification of limitations (e.g., number of culturally familiar participants) is missing, weakening the critique.
Engagement with theory in the discussion is confirmatory rather than analytical.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates an ability to relate findings to theory, identify relevant strengths/limitations, and propose justified modifications, but it remains largely confirmatory with limited critical engagement. This aligns with the lower half of Band 3â4, warranting a score of 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar chart with error bars correctly compares group means and visually addresses the hypothesis.  Axis labels and figure caption are present, but the y-axis lacks units and the title has a typo; therefore minor errors place it in the 3-4 band at 4 marks.

The student explicitly states the statistical outcome, rejects the null, and relates the finding back to the anchoring hypothesis.  Interpretation is accurate, though not deeply nuanced (e.g., no effect-size discussion).  This satisfies the 5-6 descriptor; a score of 5 reflects solid linkage with modest depth.

Both IV (anchor value: 40 vs 90) and DV (numerical estimate of Gandhiâs age) are clearly stated and **operationalised**.  The research and null hypotheses specify direction and statistical difference.  Minor wording issues ("predict lower" phrasing) keep the strand at 5 rather than 6.

Opportunity sampling is described and the choice of pre-IB students is **explained** (language fluency, availability, shared context).   Potential bias is acknowledged.  Depth is reasonable but not exhaustive, so mid-band allocation fits.

The anchoring effect is accurately described with reference to Tversky & Kahneman and the Strack & Mussweiler study.  The student then **explains how the present investigation replicates/extends** that model by manipulating high vs. low anchors.  Minor inaccuracies (e.g., calling the effect a âtheoryâ rather than bias) prevent a perfect score, but the description and connection are both clearly present.

Findings are connected to Kahneman & Tversky and Strack & Mussweiler, but the discussion is **brief** and largely confirmatory without critical engagement.  This meets the descriptor for 3-4; limited depth justifies a lower-band score of 3.

The student presents a clear, specific aim â to test whether numerical anchors influence age estimates â and then **explicitly explains** why this is important for understanding decision-making biases in everyday domains such as marketing and finance.  The link between the cognitive bias and its real-world significance is articulated, though the explanation is fairly brief rather than deeply analytical, so a mid-band 5 is appropriate rather than full marks.

Across design, sample and procedure the student provides **specific strengths, explicit limitations, and links them clearly to the investigationâs validity**.  Explanations are coherent, though not exhaustive, warranting 5 within the top band.

Controlled variables (room, instructions, time limit) and materials (questionnaire wording) are listed and **their rationale is briefly explained**.  Some controls, such as anchor plausibility, are mentioned but not fully justified, meriting a score of 3 within the top band.

Each stated limitation is followed by a concrete, justified modification (matched-pairs for culture, stratified sampling, varying time limits).  The linkage is explicit and logically reasoned, fulfilling the top-band descriptor; minor brevity keeps it at 5 rather than 6.

The independent-samples design is not only described but also **explained and justified** in relation to carry-over effects.  However, explanation is succinct and omits discussion of alternative designs or full control strategies, so a low band-3 score within 3-4 is appropriate.

Means, IQRs and a Mann-Whitney U test are used â an **appropriate non-parametric test** for two independent groups with small N.  There is a contradictory sentence about the critical value (33 vs 38), indicating minor calculation/reporting error.  Thus analysis is appropriate but not entirely accurate, fitting 3-4 with a top-band score of 4.

annotations.comment: Consider incorporating more recent empirical studies on anchoring, beyond the 2009 Negotiation Program summary, to deepen the theoretical background and demonstrate current relevance.
Table 2 correctly presents means and IQRs but lacks clear row and column headers; improve table formatting for reader comprehension.
Figure caption should specify axis units and errorâbar definition; update the graph description to fully address the hypothesis visually.
Inferential narrative notes U =16 and p<.05 correctly, but omits mention of test assumptions and exact p-value; include these to bolster statistical rigor.
Correctly reports Uâvalue and significance criterion, demonstrating appropriate application of MannâWhitney U test.
A discrepancy between critical U values (33 vs 38) appears in consecutive sentences; resolve this inconsistency to maintain inferential accuracy.

The student describes the anchoring effect but refers to it as a âtheoryâ rather than a cognitive bias, which may confuse the conceptual framework. Clarify terminology and define the anchoring bias precisely.
The summary of Tversky & Kahnemanâs multiplication task is accurate, clearly reporting median estimates and demonstrating how initial anchors influenced guesses.
The description of the Strack & Mussweiler (1997) study is cut off midâsentence, leaving the results incomplete. Expand this section to include full procedural details and outcome.
Concise interpretation of prior study results effectively contrasts highâanchor vs. lowâanchor estimates, setting a clear precedent for the current investigation.
The aim is clearly articulated and its relevance to everyday decisionâmaking biases (e.g., marketing) is explained, linking the investigation to practical contexts.
Relevance discussion mentions marketing and finance but could be enriched by explaining why those domains are particularly susceptible to anchoring biases.
Independent variable is correctly defined as the reference point/anchor condition, demonstrating clear variable identification.
The wording of the hypothesis âpredict lowerâ is ambiguous. Rephrase to explicitly state that the lowâanchor group will have a significantly lower mean estimate than the highâanchor group.
Justifying an independent samples design to avoid carryover effects is appropriate and demonstrates understanding of experimental control.
To strengthen justification, briefly compare why alternative designs (e.g., matchedâpairs) were rejected in favor of independent samples.
Opportunity sampling rationale is wellâexplained, highlighting participant fluency and availability, which helps justify sample choice.
The writeâup ignores how representative preâIB students are of the general population; discuss potential sampling bias and its implications.
Ethics description outlines consent omissions but should also detail debriefing and participant withdrawal rights to complete ethical procedure.
The student recognizes outliers and justifies using IQR to assess variability, demonstrating thoughtful consideration of data dispersion.
Descriptive section asserts mean difference but omits actual numerical values; include group means in text for clarity and linkage to Table 2.
Sentence on standard deviation ends abruptly; complete the explanation and include the calculated SD values for each condition.
Conclusion succinctly restates findings and links them to the anchoring hypothesis, effectively summarizing the investigation outcome.
Discussion section confirms prior literature but lacks critical evaluation of alternative explanations or boundary conditions; deepen engagement with theory.
Identifies independent samples design as a procedural strength in reducing biases, explicitly connecting method and validity.
Cultural background limitation is noted, but lacks quantification of participants with Indian heritage; specify numbers to substantiate this concern.
Proposes a matchedâpairs design to control cultural background, directly addressing the identified limitation with a clear procedural change.
Strength: Recognizes consistent instructions and environment uniformity as procedural strengths, ensuring standardization across participants.
Sample strengths note fluency and homogeneity, which supports internal consistency, but consider how this affects external validity.
Sample limitation acknowledges cultural familiarity but does not link this to potential effect on estimate variance; connect limitation to data outcomes.
Suggest detailing specific strata and target quotas when proposing stratified sampling to ensure balanced representation across cultural backgrounds.
